content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 26 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 222–223 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_026_page_001.jpg, DEU_026_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 158–159 — DEU_026_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_026_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And it shall be, when you come in to the land that the LORD your God is giving to you as an inheritance, and you take possession of it and dwell in it —

**2** you shall take from the first of all the fruit of the ground [bikkurim], which you bring in from your land that the LORD your God is giving to you, and you shall put [it] in a basket, and go to the place that the LORD your God will choose to cause his name to dwell there.

**3** And you shall come to the priest who is [in office] in those days, and say to him: I declare today to the LORD your God that I have come into the land that the LORD swore to our fathers to give to us.

**4** And the priest shall take the basket from your hand and set it down before the altar of the LORD your God.

**5** And you shall answer and say before the LORD your God: A wandering [perishing] Aramean was my father [arami oved avi], and he went down into Mitsrayim and sojourned there with a few people, and became there a great nation, mighty and numerous.

**6** And the Mitsrites dealt harshly with us, and afflicted us, and put upon us hard service.

**7** And we cried out to the LORD, the God of our fathers, and the LORD heard our voice and saw our affliction and our toil and our oppression.

**8** And the LORD brought us out from Mitsrayim with a strong hand and with an outstretched arm, and with great terror, and with signs and with wonders.

**9** And he brought us to this place, and gave to us this land — a land flowing with milk and honey.

**10** And now, behold, I have brought the first of the fruit of the ground that you have given me, O LORD. And you shall set it down before the LORD your God and bow down before the LORD your God.

**11** And you shall rejoice in all the good that the LORD your God has given to you and to your house — you, and the Levite, and the sojourner who is in your midst.

**12** When you have finished tithing all the tithe of your produce in the third year — the year of the tithe — and have given [it] to the Levite, to the sojourner, to the fatherless, and to the widow, that they may eat within your gates and be satisfied —

**13** then you shall say before the LORD your God: I have removed the sacred portion from the house, and also I have given it to the Levite and to the sojourner and to the fatherless and to the widow, according to all your commandment that you have commanded me; I have not transgressed your commandments, nor have I forgotten.

**14** I have not eaten of it in my mourning, nor have I removed any of it while [in a state of] uncleanness, nor given any of it for the dead. I have listened to the voice of the LORD my God; I have done according to all that you have commanded me.

**15** Look down from your holy habitation, from the heavens, and bless your people Yisra'el and the ground that you have given us, as you swore to our fathers — a land flowing with milk and honey.

**16** This day the LORD your God is commanding you to do these statutes and the ordinances; and you shall keep and do them with all your heart and with all your being [nephesh].

**17** You have declared the LORD today to be your God, and to walk in his ways and to keep his statutes and his commandments and his ordinances, and to listen to his voice.

**18** And the LORD has declared you today to be a people of his own possession [am segullah], as he spoke to you, and to keep all his commandments —

**19** and to make you high above all the nations that he has made, in praise and in renown and in splendor, and for you to be a holy people to the LORD your God, as he has spoken.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**V.5 — Arami oved avi:** This is one of the most contested phrases in the Torah. The crux is the verb oved: (a) "wandering/perishing" (adjective modifying Arami) — the father (Ya'akov) nearly perished as an Aramean; or (b) "a fugitive/fleeing Aramean" (participle). EMET renders "a wandering [perishing] Aramean" with the gloss, as this is the likely liturgical-historical meaning referenced in the Passover Haggadah. LXX Vaticanus (p. 158–159): Συριαν απεβαλεν ο πατηρ μου — "Syria nearly destroyed my father" — an interpretive rendering that takes oved as "he destroyed/tried to destroy," likely reading the Aramean as Lavan, not Ya'akov himself. This is a significant MT-LXX divergence.

**V.2 — Bikkurim (firstfruits):** The ceremony involving a physical basket brought to the sanctuary. EMET preserves "first of all the fruit of the ground" rather than simply "firstfruits."

**V.10 — Second person shift:** MT shifts to second-person address of God mid-verse ("that you have given me, O LORD") — EMET preserves this direct-address style.

**V.12 — Third-year tithe:** The distinction between the first/second year tithe (consumed at sanctuary) and the third-year tithe (distributed locally to poor) is important. EMET renders literally "the year of the tithe" (shenat hama'aser).

**V.14 — Mourning-impurity:** "I have not eaten of it in my mourning" — Hebrew be-oni (in my mourning/affliction). LXX: εν οδυνη μου — "in my grief." The verse concerns ritual purity of tithe during mourning (aninut) and uncleanness (tameh).

**V.18 — Am segullah:** "A people of his own possession" — segullah means "special treasured possession/property." LXX: λαον περιουσιον — "a peculiar/special people." EMET preserves "people of his own possession" rather than the vaguer "treasured people."

— EMET
